title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR is a security disaster and almost every cluster is a true positive — I dropped nothing. The panel strongly converged on the headline must-fixes: committed live secrets (c0, with the .gitignore opt-in c40 folded in), the hardcoded `admin-master-key` backdoor (c2), the `?adminBypass=1` auth bypass (c19/c1), eval-based RCE in the user store (c4) and admin filter (c10), SQL injection via string interpolation (c5), MD5 password hashing (c6), and PCI violations from storing/logging/exporting full PAN+CVC (c7, c42, c43). I aggressively merged the many near-duplicate clusters the panel raised at slightly different lines/categories — eval, SQL injection, new Function(), @ts-nocheck, PAN/CVC storage, password-hash leakage, admin-check duplication, CORS misconfig, user enumeration, scattered console.log, weak test assertions, and webhook URL validation/SSRF — so the final list reads as one finding per real issue. Remaining standalone keeps: JWT secret fallback (c48), no expiry/algo pin (c49), Math.random reset tokens (c50), email-suffix admin identity (c44), synchronous webhook delivery (c14), missing pagination (c15/c16/c17), 5mb body limit (c18), webhook global singleton (c12), swallowed webhook errors (c31), reset token logging (c27), and missing test coverage (c36). Severities left as-is; the panel graded them correctly.

findings.title: Secrets committed to version control
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY are hardcoded and checked into git. Once in history, these are compromised forever — rotate immediately. Secrets must never be in version control; use a secret manager (vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.) and inject at runtime via environment variables or mount points. This PR also edits .gitignore to opt these secrets in (-.env, +!.env), which is a deliberate security regression.

findings.title: Hardcoded admin master key backdoor
findings.message: Lines 15-18 contain a literal `if (token === 'admin-master-key')` check that grants admin access. This is a permanent backdoor. Delete it immediately. There is no scenario where a hardcoded master key belongs in production code — anyone who reads this repo, any CI log that mentions it, any memory dump has admin access forever. If you need to test admin flows, use a test fixture or mock the token verification in tests.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck disables type safety on auth-critical code
findings.message: This entire file is marked @ts-nocheck, turning off TypeScript on auth logic. This is a surrender — the types are telling you the design is wrong. The real issue: the eval()-based WHERE clause parser (lines 54–68) is so loose it can't be typed properly, which is a red flag that it shouldn't exist this way. Replace with a concrete query interface (e.g., `{ email: string }` or `{ id: string }`) instead of evaluating arbitrary expressions.

findings.title: eval() executing arbitrary WHERE clause expressions
findings.message: The `evalWhere()` function uses `eval()` to execute user-supplied filter clauses. This is a code injection vulnerability: an attacker can craft a filter that executes arbitrary JavaScript. Even with string replacements, eval() is never safe. Replace with a safe query builder or a closed set of named filters (e.g., `{ emailEquals: string, idEquals: string }`).

findings.title: SQL injection through unescaped template strings
findings.message: Email and password_hash are interpolated directly into SQL strings without escaping, e.g., `'${email}'`. An email like `'; DROP TABLE users; --` breaks the query. Use parameterized queries or at least escape single quotes. Better yet: for an in-memory store, just use objects directly instead of pretending to parse SQL.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing
findings.message: MD5 is unsalted, cryptographically broken, and unsuitable for passwords. It can be cracked in seconds with precomputed rainbow tables. Use bcrypt: `await bcrypt.hash(password, 12)` for hashing and `await bcrypt.compare(password, hash)` for verification. This is table-stakes password hygiene.

findings.title: Full card number and CVC stored in transaction
findings.message: Lines 42–43 add `card_number` and `cvc` to the transaction object. Storing full card numbers and CVCs violates PCI DSS compliance and creates unnecessary liability. Store only the last 4 digits (already stored as `cardLast4`). Never persist the full card number or CVC.

findings.title: Password hash returned in auth response
findings.message: Register response includes `password_hash` in the user object. The hash should never leave the server. Remove it from the response: return only `{ token, user: { id, email, role } }`. The hash is the crown jewel; leaking it weakens the security boundary.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck disables type safety to allow Function()-based eval
findings.message: The file is marked @ts-nocheck so that the `new Function()` call (line 19) can execute arbitrary filter expressions without type errors. This is another code-injection vector: admins can inject code via the filter parameter. Replace with a safe, closed query interface. The entire flexibility is speculative — build the concrete feature (filter by status, customer_id, date range) when asked, not hypothetically.

findings.title: new Function() allows arbitrary code execution in filters
findings.message: The `filterExpr` parameter is compiled into a function and executed: `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')`. An attacker can inject code. Replace with a safe predicate builder or whitelist allowed filter fields.

findings.title: Admin email domain check duplicated in every handler
findings.message: All four methods (listUsers, listTransactions, stats, exportCsv) repeat the identical admin check: `email.endsWith('@admin.com') && !email.endsWith('@honeybook.com') && req.query.adminBypass !== '1'`. Extract to a `requireAdmin` middleware (it already exists in authMiddleware.ts but is not used). Call it once in routes.ts and remove the checks from each handler.

findings.title: Module-level singleton webhooks array is hidden global state
findings.message: The `const webhooks: Webhook[]` at module scope is mutable, shared state that persists across instances and cannot be reset in tests. Inject it into the class constructor instead: `constructor(private webhooks: Webhook[] = [])`. This makes the service testable and composable.

findings.title: Synchronous webhook delivery blocks charge response
findings.message: The charge endpoint now awaits webhook firing before returning. This means every charge request blocks on external HTTP calls: if you have 10 registered webhooks, charge latency = gateway latency + Σ(webhook latency). If any webhook is slow, hung, or offline, all charges are stuck. This will fail under load or cause incidents when a subscriber's endpoint degrades. Webhook delivery should be enqueued and delivered out-of-band (via a queue, async task, or fire-and-forget with error handling).

findings.title: GET /admin/users loads all users with no pagination
findings.message: The endpoint returns `listAllUsers(filter)` directly and maps to JSON response. With 100k+ users this loads the entire table into memory. No LIMIT, OFFSET, or cursor-based pagination. Should implement page(limit=50, cursor) with bounded page size and a cursor for the next page.

findings.title: GET /admin/transactions loads all transactions with no pagination
findings.message: Calls `listAllTransactions(filter)` which does `await this.transactions.findAll()` (unbounded) then filters in memory. With millions of transactions, this is O(N) memory load + O(N) filter time per request. No limit on response size. Should implement cursor-based pagination with a bounded page size.

findings.title: CSV export buffers entire transaction set in memory
findings.message: `exportTransactionsCsv` does `await this.transactions.findAll()` (unbounded load) then builds the entire CSV string via `.map().join()`, holding the entire response in memory before sending. With 1M transactions this is OOM risk. Should stream rows: emit CSV header, iterate and write rows in chunks, use res.write() rather than buffering the full payload.

findings.title: JSON body limit raised to 5mb globally without justification
findings.message: Body parser limit changed from 64kb to 5mb on all routes via `app.use(express.json({ limit: '5mb' }))`. No endpoint actually requires 5mb: auth and webhooks endpoints take small payloads, admin endpoints are GETs. This exposes every route to DoS — an attacker sends 5mb JSON bodies to any endpoint and makes the server parse/reject each one. If a specific endpoint needs more (e.g., large form upload), scope the limit to that route and document the payload size. Otherwise revert to 64kb.

findings.title: Production admin bypass via query parameter
findings.message: The `?adminBypass=1` query parameter elevates any request to admin without authentication. The comment says 'for debugging in staging,' but there's no environment check—this works in production. A staging-only feature must be guarded: `if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'development') throw ...`. Better: remove entirely and use the hardcoded token approach during local testing, then delete before merging.

findings.title: console.log instead of structured logger
findings.message: Request body is logged via `console.log()` instead of the injected `logger`. This bypasses log levels, structured logging, and redaction allowlists. Could leak sensitive data. Replace with `logger.debug({ email }, 'register request')` or remove if not needed.

findings.title: Password reset token logged to console
findings.message: The reset token is printed to console in plaintext. This is a security risk: tokens logged to console may be captured in logs, error reports, or debug output. Use the structured logger and consider not logging the token itself (just log that a reset was requested for a given email, or log a redacted token prefix).

findings.title: Webhook delivery errors silently swallowed
findings.message: Webhook delivery failures are caught and logged to console, then the function returns normally. The caller (`paymentsService.ts` line 63) doesn't know if delivery succeeded or failed. This hides problems: a webhook might fail for hours, and no alarm would fire. Decide: should failed webhooks block the charge? Retry? Add to a dead-letter queue? For now, at least propagate the error to a logger with high visibility, or return a status indicating success/failure.

findings.title: Weak test assertion: toBeDefined only
findings.message: The assertion `expect(res.body.token).toBeDefined()` passes if the token exists, regardless of format or validity. A test that only checks 'something came back' is a false positive: it would pass even if the token is an empty string, null, or invalid. Assert the actual shape: `expect(res.body.token).toMatch(/^[A-Za-z0-9._-]+$/); expect(res.status).toBe(201);`. Better: verify the token works by using it in a subsequent `/auth/me` call and asserting the returned user matches what was registered.

findings.title: Auth tests missing critical coverage
findings.message: Tests are missing: invalid/wrong password (should fail), user not found (should fail), token validation on `/auth/me`, admin bypass behavior, email enumeration. Also: the `FakeDb` is a global singleton with no cleanup between tests. Second test registers a new user, but if the first test registered 'bob@example.com', the second test's findUserByEmail query will find the leftover user from test 1. Add `beforeEach(() => { db.query('DELETE FROM users'); })` or move to a fresh app instance per test.

findings.title: PAN and CVC logged to stdout
findings.message: `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` writes the full transaction (including `card_number` and `cvc`) to stdout. This violates PCI-DSS 3.4 (prohibit plaintext PAN in logs) and 3.2.1 (CVC must never be stored). Use the pino logger (already configured with redaction) instead: `logger.info({ transactionId: transaction.id }, 'charge_processed')`. Remove the console.log entirely.

findings.title: PAN exported to CSV download
findings.message: CSV export includes `card_number` column (line 51), exporting plaintext PAN to a downloadable file. This is a PCI-DSS violation (3.4: no plaintext PAN in any form). Remove the `card_number` column from the export entirely. If you need card reference data, export only the last-4 (already in `cardLast4`).

findings.title: Email suffix used for admin access (broken identity check)
findings.message: Admin is granted to anyone with email ending in `@admin.com` or `@honeybook.com`. Email is not a proof of identity — it's a claim. An attacker who registers or spoofs an address matching these suffixes becomes admin. Identity must be verified (SAML, OIDC, directory lookup) or controlled via a separate admin flag/role in your user store. Until then, treat this as unauthenticated.

findings.title: SSRF via unvalidated webhook URL with redirect: follow
findings.message: User-supplied webhook URLs are fetched with `redirect: 'follow'`. An attacker can register a URL that redirects to `http://169.254.169.254/...` (cloud metadata), `http://localhost:6379` (internal Redis), or `file://` paths. SSRF allows access to internal services and credentials. Mitigations: (1) validate the URL protocol is http/https only, (2) resolve the hostname and block private/link-local ranges (10.0.0.0/8, 127.0.0.0/8, 169.254.0.0/16), (3) do NOT follow redirects (`redirect: 'error'`), and (4) add a timeout and max payload size.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET has fallback to dev value in production
findings.message: `process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'dev-secret-123'` means a missing env var silently falls back to a known value. If the env var is not set in production, all tokens become forgeable. Use `const SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET; if (!SECRET) throw new Error('JWT_SECRET required')` to fail fast and loud.

findings.title: JWT signed without expiresIn or algorithm pin
findings.message: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` lacks `expiresIn` and no `algorithm` is pinned. Without expiry, a leaked token is valid forever. Without algorithm pin, an attacker can craft tokens using the `none` algorithm or swap HS256 for RS256. Use: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET, { algorithm: 'HS256', expiresIn: '15m' })` and verify with `jwt.verify(token, SECRET, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })`.

findings.title: Password reset token uses Math.random() (not CSPRNG)
findings.message: `userId + '-' + Math.random() + '-' + Date.now()` is predictable. `Math.random()` is not cryptographically secure; an attacker can brute-force or predict reset tokens. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` instead: `const resetToken = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')`.

findings.title: User enumeration via distinct error codes
findings.message: Login endpoint returns 404 'no user with that email' (line 44) but 401 'wrong password' (line 49). An attacker can enumerate valid email addresses by observing status codes. Always return a uniform 401: 'Invalid email or password' for both cases.

findings.title: CORS misconfiguration: origin: * with credentials: true
findings.message: `cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true })` allows any website to make cross-origin requests with cookies/auth headers. This broadcasts intent to leak authenticated responses to the world. Use an explicit allowlist: `cors({ origin: ['https://example.com', 'https://app.example.com'], credentials: true })`. The `*` wildcard and `credentials: true` together are a CSRF footgun.
